resource.resource_description: The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) Hazard Mapping System (HMS) Smoke Product provides the locations of smoke plumes associated with active fires in North America in near real-time. Smoke plume locations are identified through expert visual analysis of satellite-based imagery during daylight. Additionally, smoke plumes are classified by their density as heavy, medium, or light. The HMS smoke plume data is available on a daily basis from 2003 to the present.

publication.citation: US National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA). Hazard Mapping System (HMS) Fire and Smoke Product. Smoke Product.

key_variables: Smoke Plume Density Classification (Heavy, Medium, Light)
example_metrics: Number of days with heavy density smoke plume polygons overlapping geocoded address

intended_use.value: Geospatial exposure assessment for studies on shorter-term health outcomes (e.g., acute conditions) or longer-term health outcomes (e.g., chronic conditions)
Geospatial exposure assessment at the individual-level or population-level
Public health emergency/disaster planning and preparedness

strengths: Dataset supports analysis of daily variation in smoke plume exposure across North America for multiple decades
limitations: Data quality varies due to differences in cloud cover, background air pollution, terrain, and other factors that impact ability to detect smoke plumes visually from satellite-based imagery
Smoke plume detection limited to daylight hours
Dataset does not include information about vertical location of smoke plumes (i.e., smoke plumes may not be located at surface-level)
Data quality may vary over time due to changes in the underlying satellite-based datasets used to identify smoke plumes
Data quality varies due to differences in cloud cover, background air pollution, terrain, and other factors that impact ability to detect smoke plumes visually from satellite-based imagery
Smoke plume detection limited to daylight hours
Dataset does not include information about vertical location of smoke plumes (i.e., smoke plumes may not be located at surface-level)
Data quality may vary over time due to changes in the underlying satellite-based datasets used to identify smoke plumes

spatial_bounding_box: 50 Degrees West, 14.6 Degrees North, 170 Degrees West, 72 Degrees North
